summary:
Philip II of Spain (r. 1556–1598 AD) presided over the zenith and the beginning of the decline of Spanish imperial power — ruling the largest empire yet assembled, at the center of the world's most important trade networks, while simultaneously planting the seeds of Spanish decline through fiscal overextension, religious intransigence, and military overcommitment. Philip, son of [[event:2497|Charles V]], chose to rule his empire from Madrid — the first Spanish king to have a fixed capital — working exhausting hours in his study at the Escorial palace, reviewing documents with meticulous attention to detail that earned him the nickname 'the Prudent King.' Under him, Spain and Portugal were united (1580–1640), making Spain the center of a global empire encompassing Iberia, the Americas, the Philippines, and coastal Africa and Asia.

Philip's reign saw both triumph and catastrophe. The victory at [[event:2377|Lepanto]] (1571) checked Ottoman naval expansion in the Mediterranean. The annexation of Portugal (1580) briefly made Spain's empire nearly global in scope. But the revolt of the [[event:2406|Netherlands]] (1568 onward) cost Spain enormous treasure and military effort for decades, ultimately ending with Dutch independence. The failed [[event:2339|Spanish Armada]] (1588) demonstrated the limits of Spanish military reach and emboldened England and France. Three royal bankruptcies during his reign (1557, 1575, 1596) revealed the structural impossibility of funding global empire, Counter-Reformation religious war, and domestic government simultaneously — even with New World silver. Philip's Spain was simultaneously the world's greatest power and a state on the edge of fiscal collapse, a paradox that would define Spain's 17th-century trajectory.
